acoustic_nodes.description: Bull elk bugle: low guttural growl rising to high-pitched whistle, concluding with grunts. 2-6 seconds per bugle. Peak dawn and dusk activity during September-October rut. Multiple competing bulls simultaneously in river valley produce layered bugle landscape (near/far/subordinate). Frequency: 150-2,500 Hz fundamental, harmonics extend higher. Plus cow elk responses and herd social grunts as lower-intensity continuous layer.

Pacific frontal system: wind in coniferous canopy (sustained broadband roar, higher-pitched Sitka spruce vs lower Douglas-fir), rainfall on river surface (white-noise broadband), heavy rainfall on ground cover (duff, fern, grass), river flow intensification (base flow roar intensifies as storm rainfall increases river level). Three interacting sub-layers within the storm component.

Varied Thrush (the defining PNW fall voice: single sustained note at fixed pitch, different pitch, pause, meditative and fog-associated, cuts through storm layer without competing with it). Pacific Wren (remarkably long complex song). Steller's Jay complex sequences. Red-tailed Hawk morning calls. Mixed flocks (chickadees, nuthatches, creepers) continuous contact calls. Fall chorus: different from spring (resident species not migrants, mixed-flock dynamics, late-season territorial holdouts).

theory_nodes.concept: The convergence principle: E=10 through the intersection of three simultaneously-at-peak events rather than any single dominant event. The acoustic event is not the sum of three E=7 events; it is the intersection of three E=10 events in the same acoustic space at the same moment. The layers are in dialogue (elk bugle through storm wind; thrush whistle through storm acoustic layer; river rising beneath) not simply coexistent.
theory_nodes.description: Convergence principle -- intersection of simultaneous peaks, not sum of components.

theory_nodes.concept: Temporal coincidence requirements: (1) Peak elk rut: mid-September to mid-October (Roosevelt elk PNW river valleys); (2) Active Pacific storm system (not clearing, not pre-frontal); (3) Active dawn: pre-sunrise through first 90 minutes of light. All three simultaneously is rare even within the regular occurrence of each individual. The river valley geometry (reflection, concentration, canopy enclosure) amplifies all three layers and enables the dialogue.
theory_nodes.description: Temporal coincidence: three simultaneous individual-peak events in river valley geometry.

theory_nodes.concept: Varied Thrush Ixoreus naevius: the defining PNW fall acoustic voice. Song: single sustained note (sometimes oscillating slightly) at fixed pitch, followed by a note at a different pitch, then a pause. Meditative, fog-and-rain-associated, autumnal. Cuts through the storm acoustic layer distinctly rather than competing with it. The thrush is the identifying voice within the convergence -- the biological signal that locates the listener in PNW fall rather than any other acoustic environment.
theory_nodes.description: Varied Thrush -- the identifying fall voice, the biological location signal in the convergence.

theory_nodes.concept: Roosevelt elk (Cervus canadensis roosevelti): largest elk subspecies, western Pacific Northwest. Olympic Peninsula and western Cascades foothills populations. Bugle: guttural growl + high-pitched whistle + grunts, 2-6 seconds. Peak rut October. Multiple competing bulls: spatial acoustic layering (near/far/subordinate). Social dimension: cow responses, herd grunts create complex multi-layer elk acoustic event independent of storm and birds.
theory_nodes.description: Roosevelt elk -- largest subspecies, rut vocalization structure, multi-bull spatial layering.

cross_references.relationship: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Closing arc's first E=20. All acoustic layers simultaneously (SPS) paired with all emotional registers simultaneously (Ten's eleven tracks). Maximum completeness pairing.

Earlier elk-plus-storm SPS entry. Degree 349 adds the full fall dawn chorus as third layer and documents the all-layers-simultaneously convergence at E=10. The elk-storm pairing becomes the full convergence.

Both multi-species simultaneous maximum convergence entries. 349 = fall (elk, storm, dawn chorus). 351 = spring (insects, birds, rain). Both E=10 through convergence principle. The same multi-layer-simultaneously structure in different seasons.

s36_pair.pairing_note: E=10 + E=10 = E=20. Closing arc first E=20. Maximum completeness pairing. Ten = all emotional registers simultaneously active. Elk Rut + Storm + Dawn Chorus = all acoustic layers simultaneously active. Both Pacific Northwest. Both about maximum simultaneous presence of multiple distinct layers.
